title: Chewing Gum on Cotton
stainChemistry: Chewing gum is a hydrophobic polymer (polyisobutylene or chicle) blended with softeners, sweeteners, and flavorings. It adheres to cotton through mechanical and surface-tension bonding, not chemical bonding. Warm gum is sticky and smears; cold gum is brittle and cracks off. Water does nothing — the polymer is hydrophobic at any temperature. Cotton tolerates citrus oils, POG, IPA, and 3% H₂O₂ at room temperature, so the chemistry lane is wide; the key is mechanical removal first.
whyThisWorks: Cold-brittling + mechanical removal clears 80% of the gum mass before any solvent touches the fiber. Residue — the staining flavorings + dye + softener — is then handled with a citrus or petroleum solvent. NSD emulsifies the last traces. Skip the freeze and go straight to solvent and you dissolve the gum into a much bigger, stickier stain than you started with.
defaultAssumption: Fresh gum on cotton is a high-confidence DIY with a freezer and a scraper. Sugar-free gum has slightly different softeners but the same physics. Dried / old gum that's been washed already is a pro-lane job.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Put the gummed area of the garment in a zip-top bag and place it in the freezer. Wait 20–30 minutes. Polymer transitions from sticky to brittle; the gum loses adhesion to the fiber. For large garments, use an ice pack wrapped in plastic directly on the gum instead.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull blade, credit card, or butter knife
spottingProtocol.instruction: Immediately after removal from freezer, pry the brittle gum off with a dull blade. Flakes come free in pieces. Work fast — once it warms back up, it goes sticky again. If any film remains, re-freeze and repeat.

spottingProtocol.equipment: POG OR citrus oil (Goo Gone) OR IPA on a clean white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Any remaining residue from flavoring or dye: tamp with POG, Goo Gone, or IPA on a white cloth. Flip cloth frequently — residue transfers out. Never pour solvent on cotton directly.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush. Emulsifies the last traces of solvent + residue. Rinse cool.
Launder hot per care label. Inspect before the dryer.

steps: 1. Freeze the garment 20–30 min — polymer goes brittle.
2. Scrape the brittle gum off with a dull blade immediately post-freezer.
3. Residue: POG, citrus oil, or IPA on a white cloth. Tamp, don't pour.
4. NSD — emulsify final residue. Cool rinse.
products: Zip-top freezer bag OR ice pack
POG, citrus oil (Goo Gone), or IPA
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold (freezer) for mechanical phase; room-temp solvent; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Never try solvent first — dissolves gum into a bigger, stickier stain
Work fast post-freezer — gum re-softens in minutes
Never pour solvent on cotton — tamp with cloth
Ventilate — POG and citrus oils have flammable fumes

homeSolutions.agent: Freeze It Solid First — This Is the Whole Trick
homeSolutions.instruction: Put the gummed fabric in a zip-top bag (so it doesn't stick to other food) and toss it in the freezer for 20–30 minutes. Don't skip this step or try to work around it. Warm gum smears into a bigger mess; frozen gum goes brittle and pops off. An ice pack pressed directly against the gum through a plastic bag also works if freezing the whole garment is impractical.

homeSolutions.agent: Scrape It Off Right After the Freezer
homeSolutions.instruction: Take the garment out and IMMEDIATELY scrape the frozen gum off with a dull butter knife or the edge of a credit card. It should come off in brittle flakes. You have a few minutes before it warms and re-softens — work fast. If any chunk stays stuck, put it back in the freezer for another 10 minutes and repeat.

homeSolutions.agent: Goo Gone or Rubbing Alcohol for Residue
homeSolutions.instruction: Usually a faint oily / colored spot remains from the gum's softeners or flavoring. Dab Goo Gone (or 70%+ rubbing alcohol) on a clean white cloth and press it onto the spot. Flip the cloth frequently. The residue transfers out. Never pour — always dab from a cloth.
Work a drop of Dawn dish soap into the remaining spot. Dawn breaks up the solvent residue. Rinse cool.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. If you can still see color or feel stickiness, repeat the Goo Gone + Dawn round.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Old Gum or Heat-Set
homeSolutions.instruction: If the gum has already been through a hot wash or dryer (sometimes gum in a pocket gets discovered AFTER laundry), it may have melted and spread. Take the garment to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Chewing gum on cotton, age [fresh / dried / washed-through], home-treated with freezer + Goo Gone + Dawn."

steps: 1. FREEZE the gummed area 20–30 min in a zip-top bag. Or ice pack direct.
2. Scrape brittle gum off IMMEDIATELY with a dull blade.
3. Goo Gone or rubbing alcohol on a clean white cloth for residue.
4. Dawn + warm water for final cleanup.
5. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
6. Escalate if gum was hot-washed or hot-dried into the fabric.
products: Zip-top freezer bag OR ice pack
Dull butter knife or credit-card edge
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if gum has melted through a hot wash or dryer cycle. Take it to a dry cleaner.
warnings: FREEZE FIRST — solvent on warm gum makes a bigger stain
Never pour solvent on fabric — tamp with cloth
Ventilate when using Goo Gone or alcohol
Work fast post-freezer — gum re-softens within minutes

neverDo: Never skip the freeze step — solvent on warm gum spreads the stain
Never rub warm gum — grinds it into the weave
Never pour solvent on cotton directly
Never hot-dry with any residual stickiness — sets the softener residue
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates POG, citrus oil, IPA, and hot wash
Dyed cotton: IPA / Goo Gone usually safe; test inside seam if concerned
Heat-durable once the gum is off

materialWarnings: FREEZE first — the whole protocol depends on it

escalation.when: Gum has been through a hot wash or dryer and melted into the fabric, or home scrape + solvent round didn't clear the residue.
escalation.whatToTell: "Chewing gum on cotton, age [fresh / dried / washed-through], home-treated with freezer + Goo Gone + Dawn. [Hot-washed previously / not yet laundered.]"

contextQuestions: Has the gum been through a hot wash or dryer already?
How old is the gum — fresh, dried, days?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Gum is a mechanical-first, chemistry-second stain. Freeze step is the unlock; skip it and solvent makes everything worse.
